title: Green Climate Fund Authorization Act of 2025
short_title: Green Climate Fund Authorization Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Green Climate Fund Authorization Act of 2025 would let Congress spend 4 billion dollars each year in 2026 and 2027 to help developing countries fight climate change. This money would go to the Green Climate Fund, which is a program that helps poorer countries deal with climate problems. The bill says the United States should help pay for this because climate change hurts poor communities the most, and rich countries like America have created more pollution in the past. The total amount would be 8 billion dollars over two years to help other countries protect themselves from climate change.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Green Climate Fund Authorization Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has learned these important facts. Climate change hurts vulnerable and disadvantaged communities the most, both in the United States and around the world. The United States government should work with other countries to promote environmental justice and climate justice. A twenty twenty three report by scientists found that there is not enough money flowing worldwide to help countries adapt to climate change, especially in developing countries. A twenty eighteen United Nations report found that in the ten years before the report, climate problems cost the twenty nations most affected by climate disasters an extra sixty two billion dollars in interest payments alone. People and families who are on the front lines of climate change around the world are often communities of color, indigenous communities, and low income communities. These communities live close to pollution sources and environmental dangers. These same communities are often hit first by climate change causes and impacts. They also have the fewest resources to protect themselves from these impacts or to move somewhere safer. All efforts to adapt to and reduce climate change must include specific protections for communities of color, indigenous peoples, women, and other front line communities and marginalized peoples around the world. The Paris Agreement acknowledges the harm of climate change to these groups. On December twelfth, twenty fifteen, in Paris, countries that are part of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change adopted the Paris Agreement. This agreement is an important benchmark to fight climate change and speed up the actions and investments needed for a sustainable low carbon future. The Paris Agreement, which the United States has joined, says that when countries take action to address climate change, they should respect and promote human rights, the right to health, the rights of indigenous peoples, local communities, migrants, children, people with disabilities and people in vulnerable situations, the right to development, gender equality, empowerment of women, and fairness between generations. The Paris Agreement notes the importance of climate justice when reducing and adapting to climate change. It also recognizes the need for an effective and progressive response to the urgent threat of climate change. All countries must undertake activities to rapidly meet the goal of limiting global warming to not more than one and a half degrees Celsius. Developed countries have the greatest ability to reduce their greenhouse gas emissions. Developing countries have the least ability to engage in activities that reduce emissions. The ability of developing countries to reduce emissions is less than what these countries could potentially achieve. The decision about each country's fair share of activities to reduce and adapt to climate change must consider the historic greenhouse gas emissions of each country and the current ability of each country to both reduce greenhouse gas emissions and adapt to climate impacts. Developed countries that have historically produced a much higher share of greenhouse gas emissions and gained economic benefits from those polluting activities have a much greater responsibility to engage in global activities to reduce and adapt to climate change than less industrialized countries that have historically polluted far less. The only realistic way for less industrialized countries to meet their full potential to reduce emissions is through international climate financing by more developed countries. In the twenty zero nine Copenhagen Accord, developed countries promised to work together to mobilize, starting in twenty twenty, one hundred billion dollars per year in public climate financing as well as private investments and other alternative forms of finance for developing countries. This commitment was reaffirmed in twenty fifteen in a United Nations decision about adopting the Paris Agreement. The one hundred billion dollar commitment was a political compromise that falls short of the actual financing needs for climate action in developing countries. Bloomberg New Energy Finance has estimated that the transition to renewable energy sources in developing countries will require hundreds of billions of dollars annually. The United Nations Environment Programme has estimated that adaptation needs relating to climate change in developing countries may be as much as three hundred billion dollars annually by twenty thirty. The Green Climate Fund was created in twenty ten by one hundred ninety four countries to serve as a crucial financing mechanism to help developing countries limit or reduce greenhouse gas emissions and adapt to climate change. In twenty fifteen, the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change agreed that the Green Climate Fund should serve the goals of the Paris Agreement, which states that developed country parties shall provide financial resources to assist developing country parties with respect to both reducing emissions and adaptation in continuation of their existing obligations under the Convention. The Green Climate Fund is an essential institution for climate financing because the Green Climate Fund ensures balanced governance between developed and developing countries, stakeholder engagement and discussion, a balanced approach between reducing emissions and adaptation, fair and equal labor and working conditions, conservation of biodiversity and critical habitats, and strong environmental, social, and gender protections. The Green Climate Fund promotes and protects human rights and the rights of marginalized groups, including indigenous peoples, women, children, and people with disabilities. It continues to take steps to strengthen protections for marginalized groups. In twenty fourteen, the United States announced its intention to contribute three billion dollars of the first ten billion dollars raised for the initial resource mobilization period of the Green Climate Fund, but ultimately failed to honor this commitment. As of June twenty twenty three, the United States has contributed only two billion dollars in total funding to the Green Climate Fund. Pledges for the first replenishment period of the Green Climate Fund totaled nine billion eight hundred seventy million dollars, without any participation from the United States. Almost all major contributors doubled the amount of their contribution from the initial resource mobilization phase for the first replenishment. The Green Climate Fund is the world's largest and most innovative multilateral climate adaptation fund, and is a fully operational and proven institution supporting nearly two hundred projects in one hundred twenty seven developing countries.

sections.title: Section 3 Statement of Policy
sections.text: The policy of the United States is to provide climate financing as an essential part of the global effort to combat climate change. This climate financing should uphold the principles of environmental justice and climate justice. It should support programs and projects developed by recipient countries and communities. It should be designed and implemented with the free, prior, and informed consent of indigenous peoples and other impacted communities. It should promote gender equality as essential in all of the projects and programs supported by climate financing. It should include best practices for environmental and social safeguards to ensure that projects and programs supported by climate financing respect fundamental human rights. It should address both reducing emissions and adaptation as essential aspects of responding to climate change.

sections.title: Section 4 Authorization of Appropriations
sections.text: Congress authorizes the appropriation of four billion dollars for contributions to the Green Climate Fund for each of the fiscal years twenty twenty six and twenty twenty seven. Congress believes that the climate financing needs to achieve the greenhouse gas emissions reductions required to keep the planet at or below one and a half degrees Celsius of global warming are significantly greater than the amount of funds authorized in this section.

sections.title: Section 5 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, climate financing means the transfer of new and additional public funds from developed countries to developing countries for projects and programs that reduce or eliminate greenhouse gas emissions, enhance and restore natural carbon sequestration (the process by which nature captures and stores carbon), and promote adaptation to climate change. Green Climate Fund means the independent, multilateral fund established by parties to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change and adopted by decision as part of the financial mechanism of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change. Paris Agreement means the annex to Decision one slash CP dot twenty one adopted by the twenty first Conference of Parties of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change in Paris, France, on December twelfth, twenty fifteen.
